hook.rwenLine: Exercise as fitness is one frame. Movement as a mood regulator is a different one — same activity, completely different bar. Today's bar is low enough you'll meet it.
hook.culturalNote: Meta-analyses of exercise for depression and anxiety (Schuck et al.; Cooney et al.) show effects comparable to first-line medication for mild-to-moderate cases. The dose is much smaller than the fitness world implies — 15-20 minutes of walking moves the needle.

chunks.native: Twenty minutes of walking. Three times a week. That's the mood-effect dose.
chunks.literal: Movement as antidepressant: meta-analytic effect size ~0.5, comparable to SSRI for mild-moderate depression

chunks.native: Movement is the most reliable way to shift from shut-down toward regulated
chunks.literal: Polyvagal: rhythmic movement activates the vagus nerve and shifts autonomic state
chunks.phonetic: If shut-down, walk. If activated, walk fast.

chunks.native: Same movement outside has measurably bigger mood effects than the same movement indoors
chunks.literal: Green exercise research (Pretty, Barton); attention restoration theory (Kaplan)
chunks.phonetic: If you can be outside, be outside.

pattern.name: Movement before you feel like it.
pattern.explanation: Don't wait until you feel like moving — that's backward. Move first, the mood follows. The body and mind are loop, not a sequence. A reluctant 10-minute walk often produces 2 hours of better mood. The cost-benefit is absurd. The trick is to start before motivation arrives.

examples.native: Walk around the block. That's it. Don't aim higher.
Stand up. Walk to the kitchen. Sit back down regulated.
Five minutes outside. The afternoon will be different.

pairs.right: Brisk walk or push-ups — burn the energy

exercises.question: What's the lowest movement that reliably shifts mood?

options.text: Twenty minutes of walking, three times a week

exercises.explanation: The mood effect comes from much less than the fitness world implies. 20 minutes of walking, three times a week, is enough. Anything beyond that is bonus.

exercises.prompt: I don't feel like walking right now.
correct: Don't wait to feel like it.

exercises.sentence: Don't wait until you feel like it. Move first. The _____ follows.

exercises.question: Does it matter where you walk?

options.text: Outside has measurably bigger mood effects than the same movement indoors

exercises.explanation: Green exercise research consistently shows added effect from being outdoors, even briefly. A 10-minute walk in a park beats 30 minutes on a treadmill for mood.

rwenDialogue.intro: If you walked for ten minutes today — when's the most likely time?

lines.speaker: rwen
lines.rwenLine: Realistic time slot for a ten-minute walk?

userChoices.feedback: Also a great time for the afternoon dip. Set a recurring 12.30pm reminder.

userChoices.target: Right after the work day ends.
userChoices.feedback: Excellent for resetting the nervous system before the evening starts.

userChoices.target: I genuinely don't have ten minutes.
userChoices.feedback: Five then. Around the block. Or stand up and stretch every hour. Don't let perfect kill any version of it.

lines.speaker: rwen
lines.rwenLine: Pick the slot. Set a reminder. The point is the movement, not the optimisation.

correct: Twenty minutes three times a week

mission.title: Today's mission
mission.task: Walk for at least ten minutes today. Outside if possible. Don't wait until you feel like it. Notice what your mood looks like 30 minutes after.
mission.rwenSignoff: The body and mind are a loop, not a sequence. Move, the rest follows.

phase8.scenario: User has just been on a walk (or is about to be). Rwen helps them notice the mood-state before vs after, or commit to a specific time.
phase8.rwenRole: Encouraging without forcing. The user knows what they need.
phase8.successCriteria: User has named time + duration + whether outside is possible.
